title: Build Your First Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) System
audience: beginner to intermediate developers who understand basic Python but are new to RAG systems

chapters.title: Set Up Your RAG Development Environment
chapters.chapter_goal: Install necessary libraries and set up a basic Python project structure to begin building the RAG system.

sections.title: Project Initialization and Virtual Environment
sections.goal: Create a new Python project directory and set up a virtual environment to manage dependencies.
key_questions: What is a virtual environment and why is it important for Python projects?
How do I create a new Python project directory?
How do I activate and deactivate a virtual environment?
What are the basic commands for managing a virtual environment using venv?

sections.title: Installing Core RAG Libraries
sections.goal: Install the essential Python libraries needed for building a RAG system, such as LangChain and an LLM client.
key_questions: Which Python libraries are fundamental for RAG development?
How do I install LangChain using pip?
How do I install a client for a specific LLM provider (e.g., OpenAI, Hugging Face)?
What are common installation issues and how can I resolve them?

sections.title: Configuring API Keys and Environment Variables
sections.goal: Securely configure API keys for LLM providers using environment variables.
key_questions: Why is it important to keep API keys secret?
How can I use environment variables to store sensitive information like API keys?
What is a .env file and how do I use it with Python?
How do I load environment variables in my Python script?

sections.title: Basic Project Structure and First Run
sections.goal: Organize the project files and create a minimal Python script to verify the setup and confirm all dependencies are working.
key_questions: What is a good starting directory structure for a RAG project?
How can I write a simple Python script that imports the installed libraries?
How do I run a Python script from the command line?
What should I expect to see when I run the initial script successfully?
sections.builds_on: Configuring API Keys and Environment Variables

chapters.project_milestone: A new Python project with essential dependencies (e.g., LangChain, OpenAI/Hugging Face client) installed and a basic script ready to run.

chapters.title: Ingest and Chunk Your Documents
chapters.chapter_goal: Implement the document loading and text splitting components to prepare raw documents for the RAG system.

sections.title: Setting Up Your Development Environment
sections.goal: Ensure the reader has the necessary tools and libraries installed to begin building the RAG system.
key_questions: What Python version is recommended?
Which libraries are essential for document loading and text splitting?
How do I install these libraries using pip?

sections.title: Loading Raw Documents
sections.goal: Teach the reader how to load text content from various file formats into a usable format for processing.
key_questions: How can I load text from plain text files (.txt)?
What about other common formats like PDFs or Markdown?
How do I handle potential encoding issues when loading files?
What is a good data structure to hold loaded documents?

sections.title: Understanding Text Chunking Strategies
sections.goal: Explain why splitting documents into smaller chunks is crucial for RAG and introduce different chunking methods.
key_questions: Why is chunking necessary for RAG?
What are the trade-offs between large and small chunks?
What are common chunking strategies (e.g., fixed size, recursive, sentence splitting)?
How does chunk overlap help?

sections.title: Implementing Recursive Text Splitting
sections.goal: Guide the reader through implementing a practical text splitting function using a recursive approach.
key_questions: How can I implement a recursive text splitter in Python?
What are good separators to use for splitting (e.g., paragraphs, sentences, words)?
How do I manage chunk size and overlap effectively?
How can I test my chunking function with sample text?

sections.title: Putting It All Together: The Ingestion Pipeline
sections.goal: Combine document loading and chunking into a single script that prepares documents for the next stage of the RAG system.
key_questions: How do I chain the document loading and chunking steps?
What should the final output of this stage look like?
How can I save or pass these chunks to the next component?
What are common errors during ingestion and how can I debug them?

chapters.project_milestone: A script that can load text files and split them into manageable chunks, ready for embedding.

chapters.title: Create Document Embeddings and a Vector Store
chapters.chapter_goal: Generate numerical representations (embeddings) for document chunks and store them in a searchable vector database.

sections.title: Understanding Embeddings: Turning Text into Numbers
sections.goal: Explain what text embeddings are, why they are crucial for RAG, and how they capture semantic meaning.
key_questions: What is a text embedding?
Why do we need embeddings for RAG?
How do embedding models work at a high level?
What are the properties of good embeddings?

sections.title: Choosing and Using an Embedding Model
sections.goal: Select a suitable embedding model and demonstrate how to use it to generate embeddings for text chunks.
key_questions: What are popular embedding models for RAG?
How do we choose an embedding model (e.g., performance, cost, availability)?
How can we load and use an embedding model with Python?
What does the output of an embedding model look like?
sections.builds_on: Understanding Embeddings: Turning Text into Numbers

sections.title: Setting Up a Vector Store
sections.goal: Introduce the concept of a vector store and set up a local instance for storing and querying embeddings.
key_questions: What is a vector store and how does it differ from a traditional database?
What are the key features of a vector store for RAG?
How can we set up a simple, local vector store (e.g., ChromaDB, FAISS)?
What are the basic operations for adding and retrieving data from a vector store?
sections.builds_on: Choosing and Using an Embedding Model

sections.title: Populating the Vector Store with Document Embeddings
sections.goal: Integrate the document chunks, embedding model, and vector store to create a searchable index of document content.
key_questions: How do we iterate through our document chunks?
How do we generate embeddings for each chunk using our chosen model?
How do we add these embeddings and their corresponding text to the vector store?
What metadata should we store alongside the embeddings?
How can we verify that the data has been successfully ingested?

chapters.project_milestone: A working vector store populated with embeddings from the document chunks, enabling semantic search.

chapters.title: Implement the Retriever and Basic Question Answering
chapters.chapter_goal: Build the retrieval mechanism to find relevant document chunks based on a user's query and integrate it with a language model to generate an initial answer.

sections.title: Setting Up the Vector Database and Embedding Model
sections.goal: Initialize the vector database and load the pre-trained embedding model, preparing the system for efficient similarity search.
key_questions: What are the essential libraries for vector databases and embeddings?
How do we instantiate a local vector database?
How do we load a pre-trained embedding model?
What are the considerations for choosing an embedding model for our use case?

sections.title: Indexing Document Chunks for Retrieval
sections.goal: Embed the previously chunked documents and store them in the vector database, making them searchable by semantic similarity.
key_questions: How do we generate embeddings for each document chunk?
How do we add these embeddings and their corresponding text to the vector database?
What is the structure of data stored in the vector database?
How can we verify that the documents have been indexed correctly?
sections.builds_on: Setting Up the Vector Database and Embedding Model

sections.title: Implementing the Similarity Search Retriever
sections.goal: Develop a function that takes a user's question, generates its embedding, and queries the vector database to retrieve the most relevant document chunks.
key_questions: How do we embed the user's query?
How do we perform a similarity search in the vector database using the query embedding?
What parameters control the search (e.g., number of results)?
How do we format the retrieved document chunks for use by the LLM?

sections.title: Integrating Retriever with a Language Model for Question Answering
sections.goal: Combine the retrieved context with the user's question in a prompt and use a language model to generate a coherent answer.
key_questions: How do we construct a prompt that includes both the retrieved context and the user's question?
How do we call the language model API with the constructed prompt?
How do we extract the generated answer from the LLM's response?
What are common issues when integrating retrieval and generation, and how can we debug them?

sections.title: Testing the End-to-End RAG Pipeline
sections.goal: Test the complete RAG system with various questions to ensure it can retrieve relevant information and generate accurate answers, serving as a working milestone.
key_questions: How do we set up a simple testing loop for our RAG system?
What are good example questions to test retrieval and generation?
How do we evaluate the quality of the generated answers qualitatively?
What are the next steps for improving the system based on test results?
sections.builds_on: Integrating Retriever with a Language Model for Question Answering

chapters.project_milestone: A functional RAG pipeline that takes a question, retrieves relevant context, and generates an answer using an LLM.

chapters.title: Refine Prompts and Enhance Retrieval
chapters.chapter_goal: Improve the quality of generated answers by optimizing LLM prompts and exploring basic retrieval strategies.

sections.title: Understanding Prompt Engineering for RAG
sections.goal: Introduce the concept of prompt engineering and its critical role in guiding LLM responses within a RAG system.
key_questions: What is prompt engineering in the context of RAG?
Why is a well-crafted prompt essential for RAG performance?
What are common prompt patterns for RAG systems?
How can we instruct the LLM to use only the provided context?

sections.title: Implementing a Better RAG Prompt
sections.goal: Implement a more sophisticated prompt template that instructs the LLM to answer questions based strictly on the retrieved context, and handle cases where the context is insufficient.
key_questions: How do we structure a prompt to include retrieved documents and the user's query?
What specific instructions can we give the LLM to prioritize context?
How can we handle 'I don't know' scenarios gracefully?
How can we integrate this prompt into our existing RAG pipeline?

sections.title: Exploring Basic Retrieval Strategies
sections.goal: Introduce and implement simple strategies to improve the relevance of retrieved documents, such as adjusting chunking or using different similarity thresholds.
key_questions: How does chunk size affect retrieval relevance?
What are the trade-offs between smaller and larger chunks?
How can we experiment with different similarity search parameters?
What are other simple ways to filter or re-rank retrieved documents?

sections.title: Evaluating and Iterating on Prompt and Retrieval Improvements
sections.goal: Learn how to evaluate the impact of prompt and retrieval changes on answer quality and establish a feedback loop for continuous improvement.
key_questions: How can we qualitatively assess the improvement in answers?
What are simple metrics to track for RAG performance?
How do we systematically test different prompt and retrieval configurations?
What are common pitfalls when evaluating RAG systems?

chapters.project_milestone: A RAG system with improved answer relevance through prompt engineering and basic retrieval tuning.

chapters.title: Evaluate Your RAG System
chapters.chapter_goal: Implement simple evaluation metrics to assess the performance of the retrieval and generation components.

sections.title: Understanding RAG Evaluation Metrics
sections.goal: Introduce the core concepts and common metrics used to evaluate RAG systems, focusing on retrieval and generation quality.
key_questions: Why is evaluation crucial for RAG systems?
What are the key aspects of RAG performance to measure?
What are common metrics for retrieval (e.g., recall, precision)?
What are common metrics for generation (e.g., faithfulness, relevance)?
How do these metrics relate to user experience?

sections.title: Evaluating the Retriever: Precision and Recall
sections.goal: Implement code to measure the precision and recall of the document retrieval component.
key_questions: How can we programmatically check if the retriever returns relevant documents?
What is precision in the context of RAG retrieval?
What is recall in the context of RAG retrieval?
How do we define 'ground truth' for evaluating retrieval?
How can we implement precision and recall calculations in Python?

sections.title: Evaluating the Generator: Faithfulness and Relevance
sections.goal: Implement code to assess the faithfulness of the generated answers to the retrieved context and their relevance to the original question.
key_questions: How do we check if the generated answer is supported by the retrieved documents (faithfulness)?
How do we check if the generated answer directly addresses the user's question (relevance)?
What are simple programmatic ways to approximate faithfulness and relevance?
How can we use LLMs themselves for evaluation (e.g., prompt-based evaluation)?
How do we integrate these checks into a Python script?
sections.builds_on: Evaluating the Retriever: Precision and Recall

sections.title: Building an End-to-End Evaluation Script
sections.goal: Combine retrieval and generation evaluation into a single script that runs a set of test questions and reports overall performance.
key_questions: How do we create a dataset of test questions and expected answers/relevant documents?
How can we automate running these questions through our RAG system?
How do we aggregate the individual metric scores into a meaningful overall evaluation?
What does the final evaluation script look like?
How can this script be used to identify areas for improvement?
sections.builds_on: Evaluating the Generator: Faithfulness and Relevance

chapters.project_milestone: A script to run sample questions against the RAG system and report basic accuracy or relevance scores.

chapters.title: Extend and Deploy Your Mini RAG Project
chapters.chapter_goal: Add a simple user interface and discuss basic deployment considerations for the RAG system.

sections.title: Creating a Command-Line Interface for Your RAG System
sections.goal: Enable users to interact with the RAG system through a simple command-line interface (CLI) for querying.
key_questions: How can we wrap our RAG pipeline into a function that accepts a query?
What Python libraries are suitable for building simple CLIs?
How do we handle user input and display the RAG system's response?
How can we integrate the existing RAG components (ingestion, retrieval, generation) into a single CLI script?
sections.builds_on: Putting It All Together: A Complete RAG Pipeline

sections.title: Building a Basic Web UI with Streamlit
sections.goal: Develop a user-friendly web interface using Streamlit to interact with the RAG system, making it more accessible.
key_questions: What is Streamlit and why is it a good choice for rapid UI development?
How do we adapt our RAG pipeline to work within a Streamlit application?
How can we display the retrieved documents and the generated answer to the user?
How do we manage state and user input in a Streamlit app?
sections.builds_on: Creating a Command-Line Interface for Your RAG System

sections.title: Containerizing Your RAG Application with Docker
sections.goal: Learn how to package the RAG application into a Docker container for consistent deployment across different environments.
key_questions: What is Docker and why is it useful for deploying applications?
How do we create a Dockerfile for our RAG application?
What are the key components of a Dockerfile (e.g., base image, dependencies, entrypoint)?
How can we build and run our Docker image locally?
How does containerization simplify deployment?
sections.builds_on: Building a Basic Web UI with Streamlit

sections.title: Basic Deployment Considerations and Next Steps
sections.goal: Discuss simple strategies for deploying the RAG application and suggest paths for further improvement and learning.
key_questions: What are common cloud platforms for deploying Python web applications?
What are the trade-offs between different deployment options (e.g., PaaS, IaaS)?
How can we monitor our RAG application's performance and identify issues?
What are potential areas for future enhancement (e.g., more sophisticated retrieval, better evaluation, larger datasets)?
Where can readers find more advanced RAG techniques and resources?
sections.builds_on: Containerizing Your RAG Application with Docker

chapters.project_milestone: A runnable RAG application with a command-line interface or basic web UI, ready for further experimentation.

running_project: A functional RAG system that can answer questions based on a small set of user-provided documents.
